coredata.dc:title: Rapid and sensitive determination of Piroxicam by N-doped carbon dots prepared by plant soot

coredata.dc:description: Piroxicam (PX) as a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) can be effectively used for anti-inflammatory and analgesia. However, overdoses may induce side effects such as gastrointestinal ulcers and headaches. Therefore, the assay of piroxicam has considerable significance. In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was synthesized for PX detection. The fluorescence sensor was fabricated by hydrothermal method with plant soot and ethylenediamine. The strategy exhibited a detection range of 6–200 μg/mL and 250–700 μg/mL with the limited detection of 2 μg/mL. The mechanism of the PX assay base on the fluorescence sensor was the process of electron transfer between the PX and N-CDs. The assay subsequently demonstrated could be successfully used in actual sample. The results indicated that the N-CDs could be a superior candidate nanomaterial for piroxicam monitoring in the healthcare product industry.

head.ce:title: Rapid and sensitive determination of Piroxicam by N-doped carbon dots prepared by plant soot

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Piroxicam (PX) as a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) can be effectively used for anti-inflammatory and analgesia. However, overdoses may induce side effects such as gastrointestinal ulcers and headaches. Therefore, the assay of piroxicam has considerable significance. In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was synthesized for PX detection. The fluorescence sensor was fabricated by hydrothermal method with plant soot and ethylenediamine. The strategy exhibited a detection range of 6–200 μg/mL and 250–700 μg/mL with the limited detection of 2 μg/mL. The mechanism of the PX assay base on the fluorescence sensor was the process of electron transfer between the PX and N-CDs. The assay subsequently demonstrated could be successfully used in actual sample. The results indicated that the N-CDs could be a superior candidate nanomaterial for piroxicam monitoring in the healthcare product industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are mostly used to treat patients suffering from pain and inflammatory conditions such as chronic pain, osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis, and are even used as analgesics and antipyretics extensively [1,2]. Piroxicam (PX) is a potent member of the NSAID family that exhibits anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antipyretic activity [3,4]. It functions by inhibiting the cyclooxygenase family of enzymes (COX-1 and COX-2) [5]. However, excessive use of PX may cause a series of side effects including headaches, dizziness, tinnitus and stomach ulcers [6].

At present, the products in the healthcare product market are mixed. Some products may add drugs illegally to achieve a good curative effect. Consumers who unknowingly overdose on PX may have negative effects. Currently, PX quantification methods include UV [7], HPLC [8–11], electrochemical [12–14], capillary zone electrophoresis [15–17], spectrophotometry [18,19], LC-MS [20,21]. Although the above methods possess high sensitivity, there are also some problems such as complicated pretreatment process, long detection time, expensive equipment and high operation requirements. Therefore, it is meaningful to develop a method that is easy to operate and time-saving for PX detection.

Carbon dots (CDs) as a novel zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial have a typical size of less than 10 nm [22]. Most CDs were divided into four main groups based on the carbon core and attached states [23]: GQDs [24], which comprise one or a few layers of graphene, CNDs [25], which are nearly always quasi-spherical or spherical, CQDs [26], which have an obvious crystalline lattice, and PDs [27,28], which based on monomers or linear polymers. The synthesis methods of CDs can be divided into top-down methods such as arc discharge [29], laser ablation [30], electrochemical oxidation [31] and chemical oxidation [32], and bottom-up molecular approaches including hydrothermal [33], microwave [34] and combustion methods and carbonization in a microreactor [35]. The bottom-up method has become popular in recent years due to its wide source of materials, environmentally friendly, simple operation and excellent photoelectric properties of products [36–39]. The hydrothermal of the bottom-up method produces CDs through the carbonization of the reactants occurring in closed reactor under high temperature and pressure conditions [40–42].

At present, various materials were utilized as the carbon source to prepare the CDs with different properties and applications [43,44], including organics such as urea [45] and citric acid [46], inorganics such as activated carbon [47] and graphite [48], and some natural products [49] like lemon peel [50], rose flowers [51], rice residue [52]. Compared with traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs exhibit the characteristics of easy to functionalize, good biocompatibility and low toxicity [53–56]. Therefore, the CDs possess excellent application prospects in photocatalysis [57], biomedicine, sensing [58–60] and optoelectronics [61–65]. For instance, Huang [66] et al. designed a nanocomposite of CDs/α-Fe2O3-Fe3O4 for the determination of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1). Song [67] et al. explored ruthenium-doped CoP/CDs nanosheets through splicing of carbon dots for hydrogen production. Shi [68] et al. detected Cu2+ in cell by carbon dots prepared from wheat straw and developed information encryption applications by the characteristic of room temperature phosphorescence (RTP). In recent years, other element doping can introduce new surface properties and alter the structural defects of the original CDs to improve the quantum yield and the active sites [22,69,70]. Nitrogen-doping is the most studied and effective doping system [71,72]. The defects caused by the nitrogen-doping contributed to upward movement in the Fermi level and increased the electron activity in the conduction band, thus the photoluminescence properties of the CDs were efficiently increased [73–76].

So far, various fluorescence probe based on N-doped CDs have been developed. Yan et al. [77] synthesized N-CDs based on fluorescence internal filtering effects (IFE) between the N-CDs and curcumin to detect curcumin, the limited of detection was 94 nM. Nandi N et al. [78] exploited a fluorescence sensor based on the Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET) between the Bilirubin and N-CDs for bilirubin detection and obtained the limit of detection of 56.28 nM. Yao et al. [79] designed S, N co-doped carbon dots present good sensitivity to clenbuterol. The approach was realized by the amine–amine coupling reaction between the amino groups on the CDs and the antibody of clenbuterol and immunofluorescence technology between clenbuterol and clenbuterol antibody. Liu et al. [80] fabricated a fluorescent method based on the synergy of static quenching mechanism and IFE for the folic acid assay with a limited detection of 28.0 nM. N-doped carbon dots as a novel nanomaterial have been widely used to detect the content of various substances.

In this work, we use plant soot as a green precursor and ethylenediamine as the source of nitrogen to synthesize nitrogen-doping carbon dots by a one-step hydrothermal method. Plant soot is the smoke ink that sticks to the bottom of a pot after burning weeds. It contains highly carbonized products of plants, inorganic salts, and a little mineral. The N-CDs prepared with this green carbon precursor show good solubility and could exhibit strong blue fluorescence at 455 nm. After PX was added, the fluorescence of the N-CDs was quenched due to the electron transfer process. The structure of PX is advantageous in forming hydrogen bonds with the functional groups on the N-CDs surface. After hydrogen bonds formed, the distance between PX and N-CDs was reduced, which promotes the process of electron transfer. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was linearly related to the PX concentration in the range of 6–200 μg/ml and 250–700 μg/ml, and the detection limit is 2 μg/ml. Moreover, the fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched within 3 min after PX was added. The novel fluorescence method with the advantages of fast, sensitive and accurate shows an optimistic application prospect for the detection of PX in the healthcare product industry.

Ultrapure water was used throughout all the experiments. Quadrol was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Methanol was obtained from Concord Technology (Tianjin) Co., Ltd. CaCl2, NaCl, MgCl2, and ZnCl2 were purchased from Tianjin Hengxing chemical reagent manufacturing Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Glycerin was purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Acetaminophen, indomethacin, ibuprofen, naproxen, acetylsalicylic acid, celecoxib and meloxicam were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images acquired with a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) were used to analyze the morphologies of N-CDs. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded by a Nicolet IS50 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). The X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) image was obtained by Bruker D8 ADVANCE and DAVINCI DESIGN (Bruker, Germany). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained from an Escalab 250Xi (Thermo Fisher, USA). The fluorescence lifetimes were measured by FLS 1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The fluorescence spectrum was recorded with an F97 (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd. China). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum was measured using a UV-5500 PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai Metash Instruments Co., Ltd. China).

Plant soot and ethylenediamine were used as source of carbon and nitrogen to prepare N-CDs through the one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.2 g of plant soot and 300 μL ethylenediamine were added into 20 mL water. The mixture was treated with ultrasonic for 25 min, and then the mixture was transferred into a teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave to be heated at 180 ℃ for 4 h. After the autoclave cooled to room temperature, the aqueous was centrifuged for 10 min at 4200 rpm. The sandy-brown liquid on the top was collected and then filtered with 0.22-μm filter membranes. The N-CDs were collected by dialysis against deionized water through a dialysis membrane (500 MWCO) for 48 h.

The stock solution of N-CDs was diluted 20 times for further use. 100 μL PX solutions was added into 100 μL of N-CDs suspensions. After reacting for 3 min at room temperature, the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs at 455 nm were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

To study the selectivity of N-doped CDs in PX detection, the fluorescence responses of some interferences including Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Na+, ascorbic acid, glycerin, chitosan (suspension), and other NSAIDs including acetaminophen, indomethacin, ibuprofen, acetylsalicylic acid, celecoxib and naproxen were investigated. The concentrations of interferences were 0.5 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of piroxicam in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To study the applicability of the N-CDs, the liquid calcium tablets were cut up and the content was removed to 10 mL methanol. Then the mixture was ultrasonic for 2 min and vortex for 2 min. The top of the suspensions was collected, filtered with 0.22-μm filter membrane, and diluted with methanol subsequently. The standard addition method was used to investigate the applicability of the fluorescence sensor. After reacting for 3 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded under the 370 nm excitation.

TEM images were used to show the morphology and size distribution of the synthesized N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 (A), the N-CDs were spherical and can be dispersed well in water, the average particle size was 5.4 nm. As-prepared N-CDs possess good crystallinity with lattice spacing of 0.24 nm (Fig. 1(B)) [81].

The functional groups on the surface of N-CDs were analyzed by FT-IR (Fig. 1(C)). The broad peak around 3440 cm−1 originated from N-H stretching vibration absorption in the amino and O–H group [82], and the band at 2960 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of the C–H bond. The absorption peaks at 1635 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C = O, C–N. The peaks that appeared at 1488 cm−1 and 1342 cm−1 are assigned to bending vibrations of C = C and O–H, respectively. The results of FT-IR indicated that the as-prepared N-CDs contain oxygen and nitrogen functional groups such as –OH, –NH2 and –COOH on the surface, which leads to the excellent hydrophilicity of N-CDs [83]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) test was investigated to explore the crystal structure of N-CDs further (Fig. 1(D)). Two broad diffraction patterns located at around 2θ = 22.4° and 42.1° were related to (002) and (100) planes, indicating the graphene structure of N-CDs [84–87].

XPS was used to determine the element types and valence states of N-CDs. The three element peaks at 284.2, 399.1 and 531 eV were designated as C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 2 (A)). The atomic ratios of C, N and O are 69.71%, 17.24% and 13.06%. In the high-resolution XPS spectrum, the C1s band could be divided into four peaks including C = O (287.3 eV), C-O-C (284.9 eV), C-N (284.3 eV) and C–C/C–C (283.7 eV) (Fig. 2(B)). It was demonstrated that a large amount of unsaturated carbon bonds was formed during the hydrothermal carbonization process. The two peaks of the N1s band at 398.4 and 400.1 eV indicated that most of the nitrogen elements existed in the form of graphite N and amino N (Fig. 2(C)) [88]. The O1s spectrum displayed two peaks at 530.9 and 529.9 eV, representing O = C and O–C, respectively (Fig. 2(D)) [89,90].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum (Fig. 3 (A)), there are two distinct absorption peaks at 267 nm and 327 nm, which were attributed to π-π* electronic transition and n-π* electronic transition. Moreover, N-CDs exhibit a characteristic emission at 455 nm when excited at 370 nm (Fig. 3(B)). Excitation-dependent emission is a typical property belonging to most CDs, that is, the emission is tunable under different excitations. As depicted vividly in Fig. 3(C), the emission is red-shifted from 428 nm to 482 nm as the excitation was increased from 320 nm to 420 nm. The good stability of nanomaterials was verified in Fig. 3(D), the N-CDs could maintain a stable fluorescence emission within 150 min under natural light.

To achieve effective PX detection, incubation time and reacting temperature of experimental parameters were investigated. The effect of reacting time on detection was displayed in Fig. 4 (A-B). The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was quenched to the lowest in the condition of incubation time of 3 min, indicating that the N-CDs can achieve the rapid detection of PX. As shown in Fig. 4(C-D), the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was quenched to the lowest at reacting temperature of 25℃, and the fluorescence intensity would not be quenched more at higher temperatures. The result verified that the as-prepared N-CDs could perform excellent capability of PX assay at room temperature. Therefore, the incubation time of 3 min and reacting temperature of 25℃ could be selected as the optimum condition for subsequent research.

In theory, fluorescence quenching is due to the interaction between the fluorescence agent and quenching agent. The quenching mechanism are always divided into dynamic quenching such as electron transfer and static quenching such as IFE [91]. The Stern-Volmer equation was utilized to illustrate the quenching effect of PX on the fluorescence of N-CDs [92].

The equation is as follows:
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
[ Q ]

Where KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the concentration of PX, and F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs in the absence and presence of PX, respectively. τ0 is the lifetime in the absence of PX, and kq is the rate constant of dynamic (collisional) quenching. In the concentration range of 6–200 μg/mL, yielding KSV was 8.066 × 10−3 M−1 and the kq was 1.488 × 106 M−1 s−1. Similarly, the KSV was 8.461 × 10−3 M−1 and the kq was 1.561 × 106 M−1 s−1 over the concentration range of 250–700 μg/mL. Obviously, the values of kq in the above concentration range are no more than the maximal collision quenching constant reported (2.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1) [93], the quenching mechanism of PX on N-CDs can be inferred as dynamic quenching [94].

The lifetimes decay experiment and UV absorption experiment were used to explore the mechanism further. The fluorescence lifespans experiment is a useful method for distinguishing dynamic and static quenching. As shown in Fig. 5 (A), the lifespans of N-CDs were decreased from 5.42 ns to 4.91 ns, demonstrating that the mechanism of this fluorescence sensor was dynamic rather than static quenching. The UV spectrum (Fig. 5(B)) was obtained to further proved the mechanism. It can be seen clearly that there was no overlapping between the absorption band of the PX and the emission band of the N-CDs, it indicates that the fluorescence of N-CDs was not quenched through the FRET between PX and the N-CDs [95]. Therefore, the quenching mechanism was most probably due to the electron transfer between the PX and N-CDs. The hydrophilic groups of PX are easy to form hydrogen bonds with the groups on the N-CDs surface, and then the distance between the two was reduced to promote electron transfer [87,96].

Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was decreased obviously after different concentrations of PX added (Fig. 6 (A)). As displayed in Fig. 6(B-C), the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 455 nm continuously reduced with the concentrations of PX increasing in the range of 6–200 μg/mL and 250–700 μg/mL, and the liner equation was y = 2.6727x + 1.0039 (R2 = 0.995) and y = 2.8036x + 0.9488 (R2 = 0.995), respectively. Furthermore, the detection limit of 2 μg/mL (S/N = 3) was low, which can achieve PX detection in actual sample. As shown in Table 1 , this work occupies a wider monitoring range and a comparable detection limit compared with other reported methods.

Real samples are often complex, and some interferent species may exert influence on the detection of the target. To elaborate on the selectivity of the developed method, the fluorescence response of N-CDs toward various ingredients commonly used in healthcare products was studied. Some common metal ions (Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+), NSAIDs (paracetamol, indomethacin, ibuprofen, naproxen, aspirin, celecoxib) and glycerine were chosen for the selectivity experiment. As displayed in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs almost does not decrease after the interfering substances were added, while the fluorescence was quenched a lot in the existence of piroxicam. The result showed the superior selectivity of N-CDs in PX detection.

To explore the practicability of the developed strategy, the healthcare product of real sample was pretreated by the method in 2.5 above, and the solution obtained after treatment without adding PX was used as a blank. By adding 20 μg/mL, 150 μg/mL and 450 μg/mL PX respectively, the spiked recoveries in actual samples were measured to be 99.8%-102.0% with the RSD between 1.48% and 3.08% (Table 2 ). The above results suggested that the strategy based on this fluorescence sensor for PX detection is accurate, reliable and reproducible.

Overall, this work uses green environmental-friendly plant soot as carbon precursor and ethylenediamine as the source of nitrogen to construct nitrogen-doped carbon dots by a classic one-step hydrothermal method. The N-CDs present characteristic emission at 455 nm. As-prepared N-CDs possessed numerous functional groups including amino groups, carboxyl and hydroxyl, which could form hydrogen bonds with the hydrophilic groups of PX. And the fluorescence of N-CDs could be quenched due to the electron transfer process between the PX and N-CDs subsequently. The developed method detected PX in real sample through the variation of fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, and it displayed high selectivity and sensitivity for PX assay. The linear range of 6–200 μg/mL and 250–700 μg/mL and the detection limit of 2 μg/mL were obtained. The method was also proved to be applicable for PX assay in real sample, with recoveries of 99.8%-102.0%. According to the above results, the N-CDs can be used as a promising candidate for PX analysis in the healthcare product industry.

Jiayu Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Data curation. Qing Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation. Ziteng Liu: Formal analysis, Investigation, Software. Longshan Zhao: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.
